question: A surgeon plans percutaneous pin fixation along a trajectory from the calcaneal tuberosity toward the distal tip of the posterior malleolus and uses oblique axial MRI slices to map a "low‑risk zone" for pin insertion. Based on these preoperative images, which neurovascular structure is closest to the lateral border of the low‑risk zone and therefore most at risk during lateral pin placement?
ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the oblique axial images and the overlaid pin trajectory to locate the described low‑risk zone running from the calcaneal tuberosity toward the posterior malleolus. I identify the lateral margin of that wedge‑shaped low‑risk corridor on the images as the border where a laterally placed pin would come closest to lateral soft tissues.

Next, I look for the neurovascular structures that are depicted on these slices. A structure labeled "Sural N" is visualized immediately lateral to the planned pin corridor and adjacent to the lateral border of the low‑risk zone. In contrast, a labeled posterior tibial neurovascular bundle is seen more medial to the corridor, nearer the medial border. The superficial peroneal and saphenous nerves are not depicted on these slices.

Then, I directly compare these observations to the answer choices: the sural nerve is the only structure shown abutting the lateral border of the mapped low‑risk zone, whereas the posterior tibial bundle is medial and the other two nerves are not visualized. Therefore the structure closest to the lateral border of the low‑risk zone — and thus most at risk with lateral pin placement — is the sural nerve.
caption: (a) The C-arm fluoroscopic ankle mortise radiograph is used during pin insertion. The low-risk zone on the simple radiograph was transferred to the MRI to measure the distance between its borders and the neurovascular structures. (b) On the axial image, the lateral end of the posterior malleolus at the joint level (α) and the anteromedial end of the anterior distal tibial articular surface (β) are marked. The marks can be shown in every oblique axial slice using Pictured Archives and Communication System (PACS). (c)The oblique axial slice, oriented on the trajectory of the pin fixation (from the center of the calcaneal tuberosity to the distal tip of the posterior malleolus: ④), was selected for the measurement. A line was drawn connecting the two ends (α, β). Then, a line was drawn from the center of the calcaneal tuberosity (c) bisecting (m) the line connecting the two ends (α, β), indicating the medial border of the low-risk zone (②). Also, for the low-risk zone’s lateral border (①), a line was drawn from the center of the calcaneal tuberosity (c) to the lateral end of the posterior malleolus (α). Similarly, for the most medial border (③), a line was drawn from the center of the calcaneal tuberosity (c) to the anteromedial end of the anterior distal tibial articular surface (β), allowing us to confirm the safety of aiming the pin toward the medial end of the anterior distal tibial articular surface during fixation. The three borders of the low-risk zone are observable in every oblique slice using PACS. The distance (ds) between the sural nerve (closed circle) and the lateral border of the low-risk zone (①), (dt1) the posterior tibial neurovascular structures (dotted circle) and the medial border of the low-risk zone (②), and (dt2) the posterior tibial neurovascular structures and the most medial border of the low-risk zone (③) were measured. (d) Five additional consecutive oblique axial slices within 1 cm proximal from the distal tip of the posterior malleolus (④) were recorded to determine the safety of inserting the pin into the posterior malleolus 1 cm proximal from the distal tip (⑤).
sub_caption: (b) On the axial image, the lateral end of the posterior malleolus at the joint level (α) and the anteromedial end of the anterior distal tibial articular surface (β) are marked. The marks can be shown in every oblique axial slice using Pictured Archives and Communication System (PACS).
image_context: A Siemens 3.0-T MR scanner (MAGNETOM Skyra, Siemens Healthcare, Erlangen, Germany) was used for the study, and all measurements were made from the Pictured Archives and Communication System (PACS) (Fig. <xref rid="41598_2022_17490_Fig5_HTML" ref-type="fig">5</xref>). To measure the distance between the neurovascular structures and the borders of the low-risk zone, where the pin should be inserted, T1 oblique axial slices were oriented parallel to the trajectory of the pin fixation, which was from the center of the calcaneal tuberosity to the distal tip of the posterior malleolus (slice 1). This enabled the measurement of the closest distance between the neurovascular structures and the pin when it is inserted inside the low-risk zone (Fig. ). To measure the distance between the neurovascular structures and the borders of the low-risk zone, where the pin should be inserted, T1 oblique axial slices were oriented parallel to the trajectory of the pin fixation, which was from the center of the calcaneal tuberosity to the distal tip of the posterior malleolus (slice 1). This enabled the measurement of the closest distance between the neurovascular structures and the pin when it is inserted inside the low-risk zone (Fig. <xref rid="41598_2022_17490_Fig5_HTML" ref-type="fig">5</xref>c).c).Figure 5(a) The C-arm fluoroscopic ankle mortise radiograph is used during pin insertion. The low-risk zone on the simple radiograph was transferred to the MRI to measure the distance between its borders and the neurovascular structures. (b) On the axial image, the lateral end of the posterior malleolus at the joint level (α) and the anteromedial end of the anterior distal tibial articular surface (β) are marked. The marks can be shown in every oblique axial slice using Pictured Archives and Communication System (PACS). (c)The oblique axial slice, oriented on the trajectory of the pin fixation (from the center of the calcaneal tuberosity to the distal tip of the posterior malleolus: ④), was selected for the measurement. A line was drawn connecting the two ends (α, β). Then, a line was drawn from the center of the calcaneal tuberosity (c) bisecting (m) the line connecting the two ends (α, β), indicating the medial border of the low-risk zone (②). Also, for the low-risk zone’s lateral border (①), a line was drawn from the center of the calcaneal tuberosity (c) to the lateral end of the posterior malleolus (α). Similarly, for the most medial border (③), a line was drawn from the center of the calcaneal tuberosity (c) to the anteromedial end of the anterior distal tibial articular surface (β), allowing us to confirm the safety of aiming the pin toward the medial end of the anterior distal tibial articular surface during fixation. The three borders of the low-risk zone are observable in every oblique slice using PACS. The distance (ds) between the sural nerve (closed circle) and the lateral border of the low-risk zone (①), (dt1) the posterior tibial neurovascular structures (dotted circle) and the medial border of the low-risk zone (②), and (dt2) the posterior tibial neurovascular structures and the most medial border of the low-risk zone (③) were measured. (d) Five additional consecutive oblique axial slices within 1 cm proximal from the distal tip of the posterior malleolus (④) were recorded to determine the safety of inserting the pin into the posterior malleolus 1 cm proximal from the distal tip (⑤).

reasoning.claim_text: Because the sural nerve is adjacent to the lateral border of the planned pin corridor and adjacent structures are at risk, the sural nerve is at increased risk of injury from a laterally placed pin in this case.
reasoning.presence_question: Does the model's response make an inferential link about sural nerve increased injury risk?
reasoning.correctness_question: Does the model's response correctly conclude that Because the sural nerve is adjacent to the lateral border of the planned pin corridor and adjacent structures are at risk, the sural nerve is at increased risk of injury from a laterally placed pin in this case?

reasoning.topic: posterior tibial bundle less likely injured
reasoning.claim_text: Because the posterior tibial neurovascular bundle is located adjacent to the medial border of the corridor (i.e., more medial to the planned pin corridor) and adjacent structures are the ones at risk from a pin, the posterior tibial neurovascular bundle is less likely to be injured by a laterally placed pin in this case.
reasoning.presence_question: Does the model's response make an inferential link about posterior tibial bundle less likely injured?
reasoning.correctness_question: Does the model's response correctly conclude that Because the posterior tibial neurovascular bundle is located adjacent to the medial border of the corridor (i.e., more medial to the planned pin corridor) and adjacent structures are the ones at risk from a pin, the posterior tibial neurovascular bundle is less likely to be injured by a laterally placed pin in this case?

reasoning.claim_text: The superficial peroneal and saphenous nerves are not depicted on the current slices, so their relationship to the planned pin trajectory cannot be assessed from these images and would require additional imaging to determine risk.
reasoning.presence_question: Does the model's response make an inferential link about insufficient imaging for nerve assessment?
reasoning.correctness_question: Does the model's response correctly conclude that The superficial peroneal and saphenous nerves are not depicted on the current slices, so their relationship to the planned pin trajectory cannot be assessed from these images and would require additional imaging to determine risk?
